Name: curvatureawareprecisionrouting
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing for Efficient and Stable Deep Learning Optimization
Short Hypothesis: A curvature-aware precision routing framework can dynamically adapt the precision of model parameters and gradients during training, leading to more stable and efficient optimization in deep learning models.
Related Work: Existing work in deep learning optimization has explored mixed-precision training and second-order methods, but often in isolation. Recent papers like 'Tri-Accel' by Sheibanian et al. (2025) and 'FedQuAD' by Bai et al. (2026) have begun to integrate curvature information and precision adaptation, but they do not provide a unified framework that tightly couples these concepts with deterministic spectral diagnostics and explicit numerical stability analysis. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by developing a curvature-aware precision routing framework that ensures mathematical transparency and strict computational overhead bounds.
Abstract: We propose a curvature-aware precision routing framework for deep learning optimization that dynamically adjusts the precision of model parameters and gradients based on curvature information. This framework leverages spectral quantization theory and deterministic spectral estimators to ensure numerical stability and computational efficiency. By integrating curvature diagnostics with precision switching, we aim to achieve faster convergence and better generalization while maintaining strict overhead bounds. Our approach is validated through empirical experiments on standard benchmarks, demonstrating improvements in training efficiency and model performance.
Experiments: Train a ResNet-18 model on CIFAR-10 using the proposed curvature-aware precision routing framework. Compare the training time, memory usage, and final accuracy with a standard FP32 baseline and a static mixed-precision training method.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of curvature diagnostics, precision switching, and spectral quantization on training dynamics and model performance.
Evaluate the framework's robustness by training models on datasets with varying levels of noise and data imbalance, such as CIFAR-100 and ImageNet.
Perform a detailed analysis of the numerical stability and computational overhead of the framework, ensuring that it adheres to strict bounds and remains computationally feasible on a single GPU.
Test the framework's scalability by training larger models, such as EfficientNet-B0 and B4, on more complex datasets like ImageNet and COCO.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The framework's reliance on curvature diagnostics and spectral quantization may introduce additional computational overhead, which needs to be carefully managed to ensure feasibility on a single GPU.
The dynamic precision switching mechanism may require fine-tuning to balance between numerical stability and training efficiency, especially in the presence of noisy data.
The proposed approach may face challenges in generalizing to highly non-convex loss landscapes, where curvature information might not always provide a clear guidance for precision adaptation.
The framework's performance may vary across different architectures and datasets, necessitating extensive empirical validation to establish its broad applicability.
